# ARC-QUIC-API-0005 - Incursa.Quic Pending Outbound Stream Open Architecture

## Purpose

Describe how blocked outbound stream opens stay queued until a later real `MAX_STREAMS` increase makes the requested direction available without fabricating success or widening stream-management parity.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-API-0014

## Design Summary

The runtime keeps outbound open requests queued by request ID and requested direction when the peer limit is exhausted. The initial open request remains pending instead of failing fast, and later real `MAX_STREAMS` frames re-drive the queued requests only for the matching direction and only when the requested stream is truly available. The success path still uses the existing packet-protection, stream-commit, and local-completion machinery, while cancellation and terminal-state completion remain owned by the existing lifecycle boundary. The slice intentionally avoids adding `STREAMS_BLOCKED` emission or broader stream-management parity.

- specs/requirements/quic/SPEC-QUIC-API.json
- specs/requirements/quic/REQUIREMENT-GAPS.md

- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/QUIC/REQ-QUIC-API-0014.cs

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- A blocked open request must remain queued until a real `MAX_STREAMS` increase for the same direction arrives.
- Cancellation or terminal-state completion must remove the queued request so later `MAX_STREAMS` growth cannot revive it.
- Bidirectional and unidirectional opens must be retried independently against their own peer limits.
- A later `MAX_STREAMS` frame may arrive alongside unrelated application data, and only positive growth for the matching direction should wake queued opens.

## Risks

- Removing a pending request too early would make cancellation or terminal-state completion unreliable.
- Retrying the wrong direction would overclaim capacity or commit the wrong stream identifier.
- Treating the blocked case as a failure would regress the supported pending-open contract.
